text: Polarized training produces the greatest increase in VO2peak among well-trained endurance athletes compared to high-volume, threshold, or high-intensity interval training alone.
Polarized training increases time to exhaustion during incremental ramp protocols more than threshold, high-volume, or HIIT training over a 9-week period.
Polarized training increases peak velocity/power output more than other training approaches in well-trained endurance athletes.
Both polarized training and HIIT improve velocity/power at the 4 mmol/L lactate threshold, but threshold-only and high-volume training do not.
High-intensity interval training reduces body mass in well-trained endurance athletes over 9 weeks, while polarized, threshold, and high-volume training do not.
Threshold-only training and high-volume-only training do not produce meaningful improvements in endurance performance variables in already well-trained athletes over 9 weeks.
No training approach (polarized, HIIT, threshold, or high-volume) produces superior improvements in work economy compared to the others over 9 weeks.
